speaker: interviewer
text: Number six with Leslie Banos at the Simon Wiesenthal Center in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother's picture. It was taken in, I believe, in either 1923 or 1924 in Hungary. Her name was Bertha [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Banos. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In this picture, on the left is my mother, Bertha. In the middle is my grandmother, Mary. I am as a little tot. She's holding me. In the right side is my father, Morris Banos. This was taken in 1923 in Nyirbator, Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't you tell us your grandmother's last-- your grandmother's full name and your mother's full name? What was your grandmother's full name? Mary--

speaker: interviewee
text: Eincig.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, you need to say Mary Eincig.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, OK, fine. I'll do it again.  Take two.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, all right. In this picture, on the left is my mother, Bertha Eincig. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture was taken in a varosliget, which is a park in Budapest, in 1931. The right, extreme right in the picture is my grandmother. She must be in the shade. Then I am in this what they called at the time the Matrosen uniform. And the other people are friends of my grandmother, a little girl and two other ladies, whose name I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell me your grandmother's full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is-- my grandmother's full name is Mary Eincig.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and when you tell us the city, you also need to tell us the country in which the-- so when you say Budapest, you need to say Budapest, Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. OK. So it was taken in Budapest, Hungary. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This picture is my father, Morris Banos, around-- in the 1930s. It was taken in Nyirbator, Hungary in the front of our house. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This picture was taken apparently in a county fair because it says [INAUDIBLE] Pictures underneath. On the left side is my brother Pal Banos, and in the right it's myself, Leslie, or Laszlo, Banos, in a school uniform, which I had to wear in a private school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this? I mean, what year and where?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said this, didn't I?

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in 1934, this picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Someplace in Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Someplace else. I can't recall where it was taken, but it was taken in 1934. Apparently some kind of a county fair because a photographer was there. Like a Greek photographer was there taking a picture of my brother and me.

speaker: interviewer
text: In some-- near--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, near Nyirbator, Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you don't know--

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was of me. It was taken in 1942. The purpose was to give it to my Aunt Margaret as a souvenir. It was in Budapest in 1942. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is my Hungarian railroad pass. It was given to me in November 1947 in Budapest. And by the way, this pass helped me escape in 1947, December. Just two months after I received this pass, I had to leave Hungary, and this pass was very helpful for me to leave the country. Otherwise, I could not have traveled inland without this pass. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture was taken when I worked for WTAE Television as a news photographer. I was assigned, at the time, to cover the campaign to President Lyndon B. Johnson. And after the campaign was over, the President came, met everybody, all the photographers who worked with the campaign. And I had a big camera on my shoulder with a big magazine. And he made a remark. He said, I don't understand how come the shortest guy carry the biggest camera. And I told him right in the face, I said, Mr. President, you won't believe this, but when the campaign started with you, I was as tall as you.

speaker: interviewer
text: You want to tell us what year this photograph was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1964. I said this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in what city? Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember what city. We went to so many cities. I can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this in Pennsylvania, though?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. This could have been in Chicago, could have been anywhere. It was during the campaign. We went, you know, all the cities. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This picture is taken by the former President Richard Nixon in 1968 in the White House. I was taking an interview with him. I talked a lot with him, and for some reason, we became pretty good friends, with him and with his son-in-law David Eisenhower.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is that you putting the microphone on him? Who is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's me, that's me. I'm putting on the microphone on him, ready to make an interview with him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was a farewell party. It was given-- my very good friend of mine, Bishop Wright, who right after this became Cardinal Wright. This was the party before he went for Rome. It's me on the left, and the Cardinal naturally in the middle, and my wife Georgine Banos in the right. The significance on this picture is this. I mentioned to the Cardinal this, my wife was baptised in the Dome of Bamberg. He went through all the trouble and get the silver replica of the Dome of Bamberg and present it to my wife in 1969 before he went for Rome.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city and state was this photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in the Bishop's residence in Pittsburgh in 1969. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1966 in Kingston, Rhode Island in the Steelers' training camp. And two of the Steelers linesmen holding me up tried to prove their strength. Needless to say, they told me, how much longer? You're getting heavy. [LAUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Bradenton, Florida in the Pirates' training camp when I worked with the Pittsburgh Pirates. I was their cinematographer and still photographer and also helped them out in practices. I was shagging balls, or batting practice, pitching, or whatever need to be done in my spare time. I just loved to work with the baseball team.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what year this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, 1971. That's the year when we won the World Series. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my granddaughter. Kyra Elizabeth Banos was born in 1986 in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, son of Michael Banos-- daughter, sorry. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is also my granddaughter. Her name is Laura Banos. She was born 1989 in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania. She's the daughter of my son, Robert. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] There are my two grandsons. They are twins. On the left is Ian Leslie McClehan. And in the right is Charles David McClehan born in January 1992 for my daughter Karen and Phil McClehan.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this photograph taken? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in-- on Christmas 1994.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is the last picture we have, so we're going to finish the interview now. And I want to thank you once again for taking all of this time, but most important of all for coming all the way from Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania to give this testimony to us. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was my pleasure, ma'am.
